content: Date 2026-02-14. Thinking about GPS trace simplification for our Paris logistics routes — goal: keep every route under 500 points after simplification. Currently using Douglas-Peucker (Ramer–Douglas–Peucker) with a fixed epsilon = 10 m. Data: 12,000 routes collected Jan–Feb 2026, sampling ~1 Hz, GPS noise ~5–10 m. Raw points per route: average 3,200, median 1,500, some long routes up to 12,000 points. With epsilon 10 m I get median ~700 points, so still over the 500 target and long routes remain >2,000 points. Need a practical strategy for epsilon trade-offs (meters vs counts) and runtime limits — batch runs overnight. Options I’m weighing: 1) global epsilon (simple, but inconsistent); 2) per-route binary search on epsilon (gives target count but costs multiple DP runs); 3) adaptive epsilon based on speed/segment type (e.g., higher epsilon on highways); 4) alternative algorithms like Visvalingam-Whyatt which tends to preserve sharp angles; or hybrid (DP + protected points). Constraints: must preserve stops/delivery points and important turns/intersections. Target max error ideally <=25 m on highways and <=10 m in dense urban, but strict requirement is ≤500 points/route. CPU budget: want full 12k-route nightly job in under ~30 minutes if possible.
Do you want a strict per-route hard cap of 500 points, or is a percentile/average (e.g., 95th ≤500) acceptable?

I need a strict per-route cap of 500 — downstream systems assume that limit. Also must always keep stop/delivery points (typically 3–5 stops per route) and any mapped geofence points. Thinking trade-offs: epsilon in meters is the right parameter because GPS noise/background is metric. Idea A: per-route binary search on epsilon (search range 1–50 m) until simplified count ≤500. Cost estimate: DP implementation ~10 ms per run average; binary search 6–8 iterations → 60–80 ms/route; 12,000 routes → ~720–960 seconds (12–16 minutes). That’s acceptable. Idea B: adaptive epsilon by segment type — use higher epsilon >25 m for speeds >70 km/h; lower epsilon 8–10 m for urban segments with dense road network. Idea C: protect points: mark stops/deliveries and points with angular change >30° as fixed before DP or post-process to reinsert. Also consider hybrid: use Visvalingam-Whyatt for urban segments because it keeps angles, DP for long straight highway segments.
Do you want protected points enforced before simplification (guaranteed preserved) or allowed to be reinserted after a pass if removed?

Protected before simplification — simpler and deterministic. Implementation plan: 1) Identify and mark protected indices: stops/deliveries (3–5 avg), geofence anchors, and points where local turning angle >30° (compute angle over ±3 samples to reduce noise). 2) Segment trace by speed: compute per-sample speed from timestamps; label segments as urban if median speed <30 km/h, highway if median speed >70 km/h, else mixed. 3) For each route run DP with protected points and epsilon found by binary search in [1 m, 50 m], stopping early when count ≤500. For urban segments, prefer lower eps cap 10–15 m; highways allow up to 25–50 m. 4) Post-check: if after cap 50 m route still >500, flag for manual review and keep best-effort simplified version. Logging: store final epsilon, original count, final count, max perpendicular error measured, and flags. Expect nightly run ~12–16 minutes given earlier estimate.
Do you want the epsilon search to be global per route (single epsilon) or per-segment (different epsilon per segment then concatenate)?

Single epsilon per route — easier guarantees for downstream; but I’ll bias the search by considering segment types: initial guess can be 8 m + 0.5*(fraction_highway)*40 m so routes with more highway start with bigger epsilon. Concrete: initial_epsilon = 8 m + frac_highway*20 m, search upper bound 50 m, lower 1 m, max iterations 8. Also protect stops and angle>30° points before DP. For max error metric I’ll measure perpendicular distance in meters after simplification and ensure logged max_error ≤ final epsilon (sanity check). Also want to avoid oscillation from GPS jitter: before angle detection smooth with 3-sample median filter. Edge cases: very long routes (raw >8,000 points) — if after 8 iterations still >500 with cap 50 m, write to queue for a secondary pass where I allow max epsilon 100 m or split route into legs by time gaps >30 min and simplify legs separately. Secondary pass flagged for QA.
Do you want the secondary-pass automatic (epsilon up to 100 m or split-by-gap), or do you prefer these routes held for manual QA only?

Auto-second-pass is fine but must be logged clearly and kept minimal. So rules: primary pass 1–50 m with protections; if still >500 and raw >8,000, do secondary auto-pass that splits route by time gaps >30 min then rerun primary rules on legs; if still >500 allow epsilon up to 100 m but mark route with tag "auto-squeezed-100m". For monitoring: emit daily report with counts: number of routes simplified, % under 500 after primary, % after secondary, and list of routes tagged. Implementation notes: use stack-based iterative DP O(n) to keep memory down; protect points by collapsing segments between protected indices and running DP per protected interval, then concatenating. Pseudocode outline in my head: for each route — compute protected indices; compute frac_highway; init epsilon guess; binary search DP over whole route respecting protected indices; if count ≤500 done; else secondary rules. That should meet the 12–16 minute overnight window. I’ll log per-route: epsilon_final, original_n, simplified_n, max_error_m, flags, time_ms.
Ready to implement — want a concise pseudocode snippet for the binary-search DP with protected indices and logging?
